coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated cadmium sulphide heterojunction-nanospheres for the enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic dye degradation and hydrogen generation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Smrithi, S P
Kottam, Nagaraju
Narula, Archna
Madhu, G M
Mohammed, Riyaz
Agilan, R
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) developed from beetroot is used for the rational design of cadmium sulphide based heterojunction photocatalysts (C-dots@CdS) using hydrothermal technique. The crystal structure, phase, morphology and optical characteristics of the synthesised materials are determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL spectroscopy), BET adsorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and electrochemical studies. Using C-dots@CdS catalytic system, a superior photocatalytic activity relative to the undecorated CdS is observed. Among the C-dots@CdS samples, the CdS loaded with 6 wt% of C-dots exhibited enhanced hydrogen evolution rate compared with other samples considered for the study. CdS nanospheres modified with C-dots (6 wt%) resulted in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 1582 µmolg−1 against 849 µmolg−1 evolution rate obtained for CdS nanospheres within 3 h. In spite of being 0D/0D type nano-heteroarchitecture, C-dots@CdS system obtained an apparent quantum yield of 6.37 % for the catalytic dosage of 20 mg under the irradiation of visible light. CdS in the C-dots@CdS system serves as the light harvester while C-dots with discernible edges can maintain the continuous supply of photo-excited charge carriers and hence can reduce the charge-carrier recombination. Further, the photodegradation of crystal violet dye using the optimised dosage of C-dots@CdS-6 exhibited an efficiency of 97.3 % in 120 min of visible light irradiation under neutral conditions. The detailed kinetic study reveals that the mechanism of photodegradation of crystal violet dye using C-dots@CdS system can be described using pseudo-second-order kinetics. The presence of oxygen rich hydrophilic surface functionalities of C-dots, the formation of near-surface heterojunction and the suitable band structure of C-dots@CdS system leading to the optimum charge carrier separation kinetics can be attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic performance. This work offers a promising strategy to develop bio-derived C-dots based heterojunction photocatalyst to address the burgeoning energy and environmental demands.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cadmium sulphide
Photocatalysis
Dye degradation
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Studies on the hydrogen generation reaction
Studies on the organic dye degradation:

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of C-dots (b) HR-TEM of C-dots showing the lattice spacing (c) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of C-dots.
(a) Down- converted PL spectra of C-dots (b) XRD pattern obtained CdS and C-dots@CdS-4,6 and 8.
SEM images of (a) C-dots (b) CdS and (c) C-dots@CdS-6 (d) EDX spectrum for C-dots@CdS-6.

(a) HR-TEM image of CdS (b) HRTEM images of CdS showing lattice spacings and the inset displays the SAED pattern (c) HRTEM images of C-dots@CdS-6 (d) HRTEM images of C-dots@CdS-6 showing lattice spacings and the inset displays the SAED pattern.
(a) Raman spectra obtained for CdS and C-dots@CdS-4,6 and 8 (b) FTIR spectra of CdS and C-dots@CdS-4,6 and 8.
(a) Diffuse Reflectance Spectra of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 (b) Band gap determination of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 using Kubelka-Munk method.
(a) BET adsorption studies of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6.
(a) XPS total survey spectra of C-dots@CdS-6 (b) High resolution C1s XPS spectra of C-dots@CdS-6 (c) High resolution N1s XPS spectra of C-dots@CdS-6 (d) High resolution S2p spectra of C-dots@CdS-6.

(a) Hydrogen generation profile obtained for CdS, C-dots@CdS-4, 6 and 8. (b) Hydrogen evolution profile obtained for catalytic dosage optimisation of C-dots@CdS-6 (c) Reusability of C-dots@CdS-6 (d) XRD patterns of C-dots@CdS-6 before and after photocatalytic reaction.
(a) Predicted mechanism of hydrogen generation using C-dots@CdS photocatalysts (b) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 (c) EIS curves of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6.

Photocatalytic dye degradation studies of C-dots@CdS for catalytic dosages of (a) 10 mg (b) 30 mg (c) 50 mg. Concentration decay profile as a function of time for (d) CdS and (e) C-dots@CdS-6. (f) Linear fit obtained for pseudo-second order kinetic model for 30 mg dosage of C-dots@CdS-6 photocatalyst.
Comparitive data of the state of the art carbon containing photocatalysts for hydrogen generation and dye degradation reactions.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated cadmium sulphide heterojunction-nanospheres for the enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic dye degradation and hydrogen generation

Science and Commerce (Affiliated to Bangalore Central University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry/Biochemistry, M S Ramaiah College of Arts, Science and Commerce (Affiliated to Bangalore Central University), Bangalore 560054, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) developed from beetroot is used for the rational design of cadmium sulphide based heterojunction photocatalysts (C-dots@CdS) using hydrothermal technique. The crystal structure, phase, morphology and optical characteristics of the synthesised materials are determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL spectroscopy), BET adsorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and electrochemical studies. Using C-dots@CdS catalytic system, a superior photocatalytic activity relative to the undecorated CdS is observed. Among the C-dots@CdS samples, the CdS loaded with 6 wt% of C-dots exhibited enhanced hydrogen evolution rate compared with other samples considered for the study. CdS nanospheres modified with C-dots (6 wt%) resulted in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 1582 µmolg−1 against 849 µmolg−1 evolution rate obtained for CdS nanospheres within 3 h. In spite of being 0D/0D type nano-heteroarchitecture, C-dots@CdS system obtained an apparent quantum yield of 6.37 % for the catalytic dosage of 20 mg under the irradiation of visible light. CdS in the C-dots@CdS system serves as the light harvester while C-dots with discernible edges can maintain the continuous supply of photo-excited charge carriers and hence can reduce the charge-carrier recombination. Further, the photodegradation of crystal violet dye using the optimised dosage of C-dots@CdS-6 exhibited an efficiency of 97.3 % in 120 min of visible light irradiation under neutral conditions. The detailed kinetic study reveals that the mechanism of photodegradation of crystal violet dye using C-dots@CdS system can be described using pseudo-second-order kinetics. The presence of oxygen rich hydrophilic surface functionalities of C-dots, the formation of near-surface heterojunction and the suitable band structure of C-dots@CdS system leading to the optimum charge carrier separation kinetics can be attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic performance. This work offers a promising strategy to develop bio-derived C-dots based heterojunction photocatalyst to address the burgeoning energy and environmental demands.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is a fascinating field of nanoscience devoted to the development of innocuous, efficient and sustainable photocatalysts in order to address the aggravation of environmental crisis and the energy depletion. From the 18th century, we have witnessed a great deal of water pollution due to the hazardous bio-recalcitrant chemicals originating from the industrial effluents that are being drained into various water bodies. Among the various contaminants, the excessive use of organic dyes for various commercial purposes directs us to some serious global ecological issues. Organic dyes being the primary source of water contamination; can have some adverse physiological effects on human even at less dosage of accumulation [1–3]. Moreover, it is difficult to naturally remove these coloured pigments since the concentration of those will generally be less than 1 ppm in the industrial effluents [4,5]. Even though different biological, chemical, physical and oxidation based techniques are available to address the removal of organic dyes from the industrial effluents, 17–25 % of these pollutants still persist in the treated water. Therefore the requirement for an innovative strategy to integrate the overall waste water management to satisfy the human needs is to be significantly considered [6,7]. In order to meet the current societal needs as per the current scenario of energy demands, the generation of hydrogen via water splitting using solar energy with the aid of a photocatalyst is the most sought-after strategy [8,9]. Innovations leading to the development of economical, efficient and stable photocatalysts with robust hydrogen generation rate are critical to meet the energy demands. Though the research and development of solar-active photocatalysts is burgeoning, the practical applications still remain limited owing to their instability and low efficiency occurring due to the rapid reamalgamation of charge carriers in the light harvesting semiconductors [10,11]. (SEE Table 1. ).

Cadmium Sulphide (CdS) semiconductor nanoparticles owing to its narrow band gap (2.42 eV), outstanding light harvesting capability and suitable band potential have garnered attention as an efficient photocatalyst in the areas of hydrogen evolution and the organic dye degradation [12,13]. CdS being a member of II-VI group semiconductors, exhibits outstanding photoelectrochemical performances and possess unique band structure in which S 3p orbitals donate to the top of the valence band (VB) and 5 s and 5p orbitals of cadmium contribute to the bottom of conduction band (CB). The bottommost position of CB is predominated by cadmium 3d orbitals [12]. However, photogenic charge-hole recombination, photocorrosion and particle agglomeration retards the improvement of the photocatalytic efficiency in CdS. Numerous strategies such as structural/morphological modifications, band gap alterations, co-catalysts, surface sensitisations, adding solid support to the light harvester etc. have been attempted to improve the photoconversion efficiency of CdS. Carbon dots (C-dots) emerged as a novel class of nanocarbon materials with excellent photo-physical and chemical properties. Bio-inspired synthesis methods of C-dots made them a smart material of interest with outstanding applications in sensor fabrication, biomedical and photocatalytic applications. The appropriate size-range of C-dots (less than10 nm), its excitation-dependent photoluminescence, photo-induced electron transfer properties and the ability to act as electron reservoir accounts for its participation in the photocatalytic systems with extraordinary performances [14–19].

Some previous studies have demonstrated that the construction of C-dots based heterojunction can have the advantages of directing the flow of photo generated charge carriers by virtue of its electron accepting and transport properties to achieve elevated photocatalytic performances [20–25]. Hence the strategic design of a novel photocatalyst is significant where the advantages of C-dots and CdS are coined to achieve superior photocatalytic efficiency. For instance, CdS nanoparticles combined with MoS2 has been reported by Xu et al. which owing to the enhanced absorption of visible light and effective charge-carrier (electron-hole) separation resulted in the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [26]. Wang et al. detailed in their study about the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) based CdS heterojunction where the crystallinity of g-C3N4 has been controlled to modify the photocatalytic ability [27]. Our group had recently evaluated the role of MoS2 and WS2 as co-catalysts in CdS nanorods and reported that the CdS-WS2 exhibited superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency [28]. Sawant et al. reported carbon modified CdS nanoparticles with an elevated photocatalytic degradation performance shown against Rhodamine B dye [29]. Recently, Shi et al. used N doped C-dots combined with CdS for the evaluation of near IR light mediated photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [30]. From the literature, it is evident that the photocatalytic performances of CdS based systems are highly dependent on the morphology, crystal phase, exposure of the facets, interfacial junctions and co-catalysts.

In this work, we have achieved the fabrication of C-dots@CdS hybrid photocatalysts via a simple hydrothermal technique. C-dots synthesised from beetroot add additional advantage of environmental friendliness to its credit. The as synthesised photocatalysts exhibited a superior performance in the visible light assisted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution as well as crystal violet dye degradation the experiments compared to the pristine CdS nanoparticles. To the best of our awareness, no relevant reports are available on the biogenic C-dots incorporated CdS (C-dots@CdS) hybrid nano-photocatalyst to address both the aforementioned environmental and energy applications.

Beetroot has been procured from a market near BEL road, Bangalore. Crystal violet dye is purchased from Sigma Aldrich (India). Cadmium nitrate, thiourea, ammonia, ethanol and poly ethylene glycol are purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory (India). All the chemicals/reagents used throughout this study are of analytical standard and are used as such as it was procured. The synthesis is carried out in double distilled water (DDW).

XRD analysis is carried out with the aid of Bruker D8 Advance Diffractometer equipped with with Cu K alpha radiation with a wavelength of 0.15406 nm. TEM is performed using Joel/JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope. The surface morphology was observed using a scanning electron microscope (make: TESCAN-MIRA 3 LMH field emission scanning electron microscope) and the elemental composition is investigated by EDS QUANTAX 200 attached with X-Flash Bruker energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer. Raman spectra are obtained from Peak Seeker Pro TM enabled with a laser power of 5–300 mW (wavelength-785 nm). UV-DRS are recorded using Specord 210 plus Analytic Jena UV–Visible spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence studies are carried out using Hitachi F 2700 spectrofluorimeter maintaining 5 nm slit width uniformly at 1500 nm min−1 scan rate. FTIR is obtained from KBr disks using Perkin Elmer Spectrum 1000 at an operating range of 350–4000 cm−1. X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Thermo Scientific, MULTLAB 2000 base system and BET studies using BELSORP-max. Electrochemical impedance spectral studies are performed using a standard three electrode cell with one platinum wire as the reference while the saturated calomel electrode acted as the counter electrode in the electrochemical workstation.

C-dots have been obtained from red beetroot as the starting material. The procedure is as per our previous report [31]. Briefly, beetroot juice was obtained using a domestic mixer grinder from freshly peeled beetroot from which 60 mL is poured to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept for reaction at 160 0C for eight hours. After cooling down, the resultant dark-brown solution was subjected to filtration through a membrane with 0.2 µm pore diameter. The C-dots obtained are washed repeatedly with ethanol to remove unwanted organic moieties and double distilled water by centrifuging at 10000 rpm. Later, the resultant product is dialysed overnight against distilled water using a dialysis membrane. Sticky-natured dried product is obtained by drying the sample in a vacuum oven at 60 0C.

0.5 g cadmium nitrate and 1.5 g thiourea were dissoluted in 37.5 mL of DDW separately. Then, the individual reactant solutions are mixed slowly and magnetically stirred for 30 min. 1 mL PEG solution was added drop wise to the reactant mixture and the stirring continued for another 30 min. pH of the resultant solution was maintained at 12 by the addition of NH3 to the mixture. The obtained solution is transferred into 100 mL autoclave maintained at a temperature of 140 0C overnight. Once the autoclave attained room temperature, the yellow solid particles obtained were washed thoroughly with DDW and ethanol to remove excess thiourea and other byproducts via centrifugation (10000 rpm for 10 min). The obtained residue was dried at 80 0C for 4 h and calcined at 300 0C for 1 h.

The synthesis of C-dots@CdS sample is carried out by the addition of 4, 6 and 8 wt% of C-dots along with the reagents in the initial stage prior to the addition of surfactant which will be designated as C-dots@CdS-4, 6 and 8 respectively. The rest of the procedure is same as above.

The hydrogen evolution studies assisted with visible light and photocatalyst were carried out in a quartz reactor having 130 mL capacity equipped with water circulation to maintain the temperature variations. Typically, 10 mg of the catalyst and 75 mL of water were added to the reactor and subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min to obtain a homogenous dispersion of nanocatalyst. A mixture of Na2S and Na2SO3 were used as the sacrificial agents for hole scavenging at an approximate pH of 13. The photo-reactor was de-aerated to remove oxygen by purging N2 gas for 10 min. Later the reactor was irradiated with UV–Visible light source, 400 W Xenon lamp and the evolved hydrogen was quantified by injecting 5 mL at regular interval of time using a gas-tight syringe by Perkin Elmer Clarus 580 GC chromatograph with thermal conductivity detector.

Photodegradation studies of organic dye were carried out using an indigenous photoreactor equipped with visible source, 125 W mercury vapour lamp. Typically, 10 mg of the catalyst was added to 10 ppm concentration of the dye and subjected to magnetic agitation for 30 min in dark conditions to reach equilibrium of adsorption and desorption between the organic dye and photocatalyst under study. Later, model pollutant solution was irradiated with visible rays while subjecting to continuous agitation. At regular intervals, 2 mL of sample was carefully withdrawn and subjected to centrifugation. Then the concentration of model dye is determined by continuously monitoring the maximum absorption intensity observed at 592 nm. The efficiency of dye degradation using the photocatalyst is calculated using the following equation.
Degradation efficiency (%) = (1-A/A0) X 100(1).

HR-TEM images of C-dots suggests (Fig. 1
(a) and (b)) that they acquired a quasi-spherical morphology with slight agglomeration having size less than 5 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon atom (Fig. 1 (b)). To analyse the optical characteristics of C-dots, UV–Visible absorption spectra has been carried out (Fig. 1 (c)). The intense absorption maxima obtained at 282 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of -CC in the sp2 carbon atoms and the broad shoulder peak indicates n-π* transition of amino (–NH2) and carbonyl (-CO) functional groups present in the system [32,33]. Raman spectrum of C-dots (Fig. S1) displays two characteristic bands; one centered at 1320 cm−1 ascribed to the sp3 carbon atoms originated due to the A1g symmetry photons (d-band) and the one centered at 1660 cm−1 due to the E2g symmetry photons of sp2 hybridised carbon atom (G-band).

The down converted PL spectra of C-dots is depicted in the Fig. 2
(a) and it is clear that C-dots derived from beetroot exhibited its characteristic ‘excitation wavelength dependent emission’ spectral nature [34,35]. At an excitation wavelength of 345 nm, an emission peak is observed at 480 nm. On altering the excitation wavelength, the emission peak maxima consequently appeared to have red-shifted. The most intense emission is obtained at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm and the intensity started to decrease thereafter. The presence of surface functional moieties and its percentage of surface oxidation can account for this particular behaviour. The increased number of surface energy traps will end up with the clutching of more excitons resulting in the enhanced rate of recombination leading to the observed red-shift [36,37]. The FTIR spectrum of C-dots evinces two strong bands centred at 3410 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 ascribed to the hydroxyl and amino functionalities present in the system. The bands corresponding to the stretching of the nitrile group and the carbonyl groups are observed at 2100 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 respectively. Additional bands imply the presence of –CC (1403 cm−1), -NH (1085 cm−1) and -CH (624 cm−1) bonds (Fig. S2) [34,38].

The X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 2 (b)) for CdS displays diffraction peaks at 2θ values corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (212) diffraction planes which can be indexed well for the hexagonal wurtzite phase of CdS (JCPDS No. 65–3414). The nature of the peak evidenced the crystalline nature of the sample with its most intense diffraction peak ascribed to the (002) diffraction plane denotes the preferential growth along [001] hexagonal crystallites of CdS [39,40]. In the C-dots@CdS samples, a less intense broad peak emerged at around 22.2 0 indicating the presence of C-dots in C-dots@CdS-6 and 8 samples. Probably due to the lower weight percent of C-dots in the modified sample, the peak was not evident in C-dots@CdS-4. But the hexagonal phase obtained for the pristine CdS is not affected by the inclusion of C-dots. Moreover, the diffraction peaks appear slightly shifted towards the right elucidating the effective integration of C-dots within the CdS matrix. The hexagonal wurtzite phase of CdS is reported to exhibit elevated photocatalytic performances once compared with the other phases and exhibit excellent stability in the room temperature [12].

SEM images of C-dots, CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 has been provided in Fig. 3 . The SEM images of C-dots (see Fig. 3 (a)) reveal highly agglomerated clusters of nanospheres while pristine CdS nanoparticles portray the polydispersed, non-uniformly sized nanoclusters with agglomeration (Fig. 3 (b)). It is to be noted that the inclusion of C-dots in CdS resulted in the uniform size distribution with well-defined spherical morphology as evident from Fig. 3 (c). The EDAX spectral signals of C-dots@CdS-6 (Fig. 3 (d)) point out the elemental determination of carbon, cadmium and sulphur against the appearance of cadmium and sulphur in the pristine CdS (Fig. S3). This further confirms the incorporation of carbon dots into the CdS system.

HR-TEM investigation suggests that the CdS (Fig. 4
(a)) acquired a spheroidal shape with high agglomeration. The size range of the synthesised nanomaterials was found to be 20–40 nm. The selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) obtained for CdS exhibits well differentiated lattice fringes corroborating the generation of crystalline particles (see the inset of Fig. 4 (b)). The resolved lattice fringes shows a crystal lattice spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. 4 (b)) which corresponds to the (111) reflection plane of CdS [41]. HRTEM analysis of CdS modified with C-dots reveal that the consolidation of C-dots had not affected the size of the CdS nanomaterials (Fig. 4 (c)). The individual analysis of the particles show that the C-dots entered into the CdS matrix to form the morphology similar to core–shell nanostructure. The SAED pattern implies the formation of the well crystalline nanoparticles (see the inset of Fig. 4 (d)). To further ascertain this observation, the resolved lattice fringes shown in the Fig. 4 (d) were carefully analysed. The lattice spacing observed in the core region of C-dots@CdS-6 system was found to be 0.21 nm which can be indexed to the (100) reflection plane of C-dots while the spacing observed at the shell region corresponds to 0.34 nm ascribed to the (111) lattice reflection plane of CdS. This intimate vicinity or the heterojunction formed between C-dots and CdS generates active sites which can be assumed to provide aid in the photocatalytic ability enhancement [42].

Fig. 4 (a) HR-TEM image of CdS (b) HRTEM images of CdS showing lattice spacings and the inset displays the SAED pattern (c) HRTEM images of C-dots@CdS-6 (d) HRTEM images of C-dots@CdS-6 showing lattice spacings and the inset displays the SAED pattern.

The Raman spectra performed on pristine CdS portrayed two characteristic and significant bands for the hexagonal CdS (Fig. 5
(a)). The band centered at around 306 cm−1 corresponds to the first order and 608 cm−1 corresponds to the second order longitudinal optical modes of CdS in hexagonal phase [43]. During the inspection of the Raman spectra for C-dots@CdS systems, it is found that all the modified samples other than C-dots@CdS-4 as shown in the Fig. 5 (a) exhibited all the bands corresponding to the hexagonal CdS and C-dots. More specifically, the peak obtained at 306 cm−1 and 608 cm−1 are respectively due to the first and second order longitudinal optical modes of hexagonal cadmium sulfide nanoparticles while the d-band occurred at around 1320 cm−1 demonstrated the defective carbon frame work formed out of the aerial oxidation of the surface functionalities and the G-band centered at around 1660 cm−1 signifies the network of sp2 hybridised carbon. However, the intensity of the d-band and G-band remain decreased in the case of C-dots@CdS-6,8 samples probably owing to the low weight loading of C-dots. Anyhow, the investigation of Raman spectra strongly concur the successful formation of C-dots modified CdS nanoheterostructures. To further understand the chemical composition and the functional groups present in the system, FTIR studies were carried out (Fig. 5 (b)). In the IR spectrum of pristine CdS, vibrational bands at 3460 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 occurred due to water molecules adsorbed on to the CdS nanoparticles. The remaining bands are characteristic of Cd-S bond stretching [44]. In the vibrational spectrum of C-dots@CdS-6,8 heterostructures, some additional bands corresponding to the functional moieties such as –OH, –NH2, CO, CC etc. of C-dots are also observed along with the characteristic Cd-S stretching bands.

The diffuse reflectance spectral measurements of both CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 presented in the Fig. 6
(a) depicts that both the samples inherit strong photoabsorption in the visible region. The pure CdS exhibited a sharp light absorption edge approximately at 420 nm owing to its intrinsic band gap absorption [45] whereas the C-dots@CdS-6 sample evinced an appreciable red-shifted absorption edge at around 480 nm. This widening of the optical absorption band as a result of the incorporation of C-dots into the system can result in the improved photocatalytic performances of the modified sample. The band energies of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 were computed using Kubelka-Munk relation (See Fig. 6 (b)). The plot of (F(R)hν)1/2 against hν, yields the direct band gap of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 as 2.02 eV and 1.78 eV respectively. Further, to investigate the surface charge of the synthesised composite, the conventional pH drift method was used as reported in [46] to find out the point of zero charge (pHPZC) value (Fig. S4). The pHPZC value was found to be 4.5 which indicate that below this point, the superficial charge of the catalyst be positively charged and above this pH, the surface charge tends to be negative.

BET N2 adsorption–desorption studies have been carried out to understand the change in surface properties emerged as a result of incorporation of C-dots into CdS matrix (Fig. 7
(a) and (b)). The BET studies reveal that the surface area of C-dots@CdS-6 sample is 92.8 m2g−1 which is considerably more when compared with that of pristine CdS with surface area of 78.2 m2g−1. This can be ascribed to the reduction in agglomeration observed in the C-dots@CdS-6 as evident from the SEM and TEM analysis.

The surface composition and oxidation states of the elements in C-dots@CdS-6 were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis and the results shown in Fig. 8 . The survey scan for the C-dots@CdS-6 material reveals the presence of C, N, Cd and S ( Fig. 8 (a)). The C 1 s core-level spectrum ( Fig. 8 (b)) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 282.4 eV, 284.5 eV and 286.5 eV, which correspond to CC, CN, and CO are present in the C-dots@CdS-6 respectively. Fig. 8 (c) shows the Cd 3d spectra and it exhibits two major peaks at 403.2 eV (Cd 3d5/2) and 410.1 (Cd 3d3/2) eV, signifying the presence of Cd2+ state in the C-dots@CdS-6. Inset of Fig. 8 (c) shows N 1 s spectrum with low peak intensity situated at 401.4 eV could be arises to NH in C-dots@CdS-6. Further, the deconvolution of the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 8 (d)) yields two doublets peaks at 161 eV and 167 eV associated with S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively are consistent with the S 2p of CdS in C-dots@CdS. Hence, the XPS analysis further confirms the successful formation of composite C-dots@CdS sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the hydrogen generation reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic hydrogen generation measurements under visible irradiation (λ > 395 nm) were carried out using Na2S and Na2SO3 as sacrificial agents to consume photogenerated holes by supplying sacrificial electrons in the system. The addition of 20 mg each of C-dots@CdS-4,6,8 systems resulted in the evolution of 920.6 µmolg−1, 1582 µmolg−1 and 1240.5 µmolg−1 of hydrogen against 849 µmolg−1 obtained for pristine CdS in three hours (Fig. 9
(a)). This observation conveys the fact that the loading of 6 wt% of C-dots in CdS matrix have minimised the electron-hole recombination to its best. In order to optimise the catalytic dosage, the experiment is repeated using 10 mg and 30 mg of C-dots@CdS-6 catalyst where 868 and 649 µmolg−1 of hydrogen evolution rates are observed with in three hour duration (Fig. 9 (b)). Hence 20 mg of C-dots@CdS-6 is the optimised catalytic dosage for 75 mL of water with an apparent quantum yield of 6.37 % (See Fig S5). These observations clearly validate the incorporation of C-dots into CdS nanospheres supports the effective separation of electron and holes in CdS entity to improve its visible light driven photocatalytic ability. Additionally the reusability of the photocatalyst has been checked for repeated cycles (Fig. 9 (c)). It is found that the hydrogen evolution remained almost same for the first two cycles. But found to have reduced in the third cycle probably owing to the oxidation of radical scavenger during the catalytic reaction. To validate the speculation, the fourth cycle has been run after the replenishment of scavenger (3 mL) to the system. It has been noted that the hydrogen evolution rate has been restored after the replenishment of scavenger. This confirms that the incessant availability of hole scavengers in the catalytic system is mandatory for maintaining the hydrogen production efficiency. To check the stability of the photocatalyst, XRD studies have been carried out after the recyclability test (Fig. 9 (d)). The results show the outstanding stability of the synthesised photocatalyst. The mechanism of hydrogen generation is as shown in Fig. 10
(a). The visible light irradiation resulted in the generation of photogenerated charge carriers in the C-dots@CdS. The existence of sacrificial agents in the system helps in the consumption of holes while C-dots help in the effective absorption of visible light. The π conjugated structure and oxygen rich functionalities in C-dots can lead to the complexation with CdS semiconductor matrix [30]. Therefore it works as an electronic buffer which facilitates the continuous availability of electrons for the contiguous CdS semiconductor with proper band edge positioning to generate H2 from H+.

To further ascertain the role played by C-dots in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of C-dots@CdS-6 system, the PL spectra of both the samples are analysed at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm (Fig. 10 (b)). Emission spectra obtained for both the samples clearly shows the exciton luminescence peak and the peaks due to the electronic transition from the conduction band to an acceptor level which arise from the interstitial Sulphur atoms (trapped luminescence). But the intensity of the peaks obtained for C-dots@CdS-6 system is considerably less compared to pristine CdS which suggests the low charge-carrier recombination rate in the C-dots@CdS-6 [30]. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectral (EIS) measurements were performed to understand the efficiency of separation and transfer of electrons and holes that are photogenerated. If a smaller semicircle radius is observed in the EIS Nyquist plot, a lower electron transfer resistance is offered by the system [47]. Nyquist plot of pure CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 samples shown in the Fig. 10 (c) clearly shows that the radius of C-dots@CdS is smaller compared to the pristine CdS. This further ascertains that the inclusion of C-dots resulted in the effective interfacial charge carrier transfer and enhances their separation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the organic dye degradation:
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance evaluation has also been carried out for the C-dots@CdS-6 material and has been compared with that of pristine CdS using crystal violet (CV) dye as the target molecule. The point of zero charge measurement revealed that the surface charge of C-dots@CdS tends to be negative under neutral condition and hence it holds advantage of adsorbing positively charged moieties. CV dye is a cationic dye which is a triarylmethane based molecule used in many textile, printing, paper and leather industries [3]. Primarily, we have carried out a blank experiment to study the self-degradation of the dye under visible light for 45 min which resulted in almost negligible degradation. The attenuation of absorption intensity was noted at a wavelength of 592 nm of CV dye to understand the degradation process. For the pollutant model of 10 mg/L concentration, 10 mg each of CdS and C-dots@CdS-6 photocatalyst have been added and the decrease in absorption intensity has been noted under visible light irradiation. A dark field experiment to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium for 30 min has been carried out in all studies before subjecting the system to visible wavelength. It is observed that the degradation efficiency of 76.4 % is obtained for pristine CdS while the usage of C-dots@CdS resulted in 84.27 %. In order to optimise the catalytic dosage to obtain efficient dye degradation, the experiment is repeated with 30 and 50 mg catalytic dosages of both the photocatalysts. It is evident from Fig. 11 (a-c) that 30 mg C-dots@CdS catalytic dosage resulted in 97.32 % degradation against 88.46 % shown for bare CdS (Fig. S6(b). The dye degradation studies using pure CdS nanoparticles with different catalytic dosages have been provided in Fig. S6(a-c). In both the cases, the degradation efficiency decreased on increasing the dosage of catalyst beyond 30 mg. This can be attributed to the agglomeration of photocatalyst leading to the hindered visible light absorption resulting in the decreased degradation rate. Fig. 11(d-e) displays the concentration declination profile of CV dye with respect to time in the presence of CdS and C-dots@CdS respectively. In order to understand the kinetics and to obtain some insight of the mechanism of dye degradation for the optimised catalytic loading, Langmuir-Hinshelwood and pseudo-second order kinetic models have been used. Langmuir-Hinshelwood otherwise known as pseudo-first order model is explained by the equation, ln (C/C0) = -Kappt, where C0 is the initial concentration and C, the final concentration of dye at a time t (Fig. S6). The pseudo-second order kinetics was evaluated using the equation (1/C)-(1/C0) = Kappt, where C0 and C are respectively the initial and final concentrations of the dye. A linear fit between ln (1/C) against t shown in Fig. 11 (f) when compared with Fig. S7 reveals that the degradation process follows pseudo-second order kinetics. The study has also been carried out in dyes of varying concentration (10 ppm, 15 ppm, 20 ppm and 25 ppm) using the optimised catalytic dosage. It has been found that the percentage of dye degradation efficiency decreased slightly with increase in dye concentration (See fig. S8). As the dye concentration increased, the number of active sites available on the catalyst would be decreased limiting the number of hydroxyl and superoxide anion radicals generated. The dependence of degradation efficiency with respect to pH (pH-4, 6 and 8) was also analysed (fig. S9). It is found that the degradation efficiency reduced under acidic pH while elevated with increase in the basicity. In acidic pH, protonation of dye as well as the photocatalyst may occur owing to which the dye molecules may face repulsion from the photocatalytic reaction medium. At the same time in basic pH, the availability of hydroxyl radicals will be substantially increased and there by elevating the degradation efficiency as well. The reusability of C-dots@CdS photocatalyst has been checked by adding 30 mg catalyst for 120 min for every freshly prepared 10 mg mL−1 CV dye (Fig. S10(a)). After every cycle, the photocatalyst was recovered via centrifugation followed by repeated washing with distilled water. Up to three consequent cycles, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency exhibited by C-dots@CdS system remained unaltered. Moreover, radical scavenging studies were performed to study the roles played by the reactive species such as . OH, O2
.- and h+ using specific scavengers namely isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and ammonium oxalate (AO) of 1 mM concentration respectively (Fig. S10(b)) [3]. The addition of AO and IPA separately in the photo treatment experiments resulted in 7 % and 12.3 % decrease in the degradation efficiency compared with the control. Interestingly, the addition of BQ to the CV dye photodegradation system resulted in the suppression of degradation up to 58 % which suggests the prominent role played by the O2
.- in the observed degradation process once compared with h+ and . OH.

Based on the obtained results, a probable mechanism of dye degradation can be suggested as follows: From the pH drift method, the surface charge of the C-dots@CdS photocatalyst is found to be negative under neutral conditions which could have resulted in the effective adsorption of the cationic CV dye on to the surface followed by the photodegradation. The photoexcited CV dye (CV*) are capable of transferring photoinduced electrons to the conduction band of CdS owing to the positioning of conduction band potential of CdS and the oxidation band potential of CV dye [47]. In addition to this, the direct photoexcitation of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band of the semiconductor is also supposed to happen on the irradiation of visible light. The predominance of C-dots in the system is that they can capture and transport these photoinduced electrons to aid the restriction of charge-carrier recombination to augment the redox reaction. The negatively charged photogenerated electrons react with the surface adsorbed water molecules to produce superoxide anion radical, O2
.- while the holes react with H2O to produce . OH radicals. Ultimately, all the active species possessing high oxidising ability generated in the reaction system will degrade CV dye to small molecules or CO2 and water. The role of C-dots in enhancing the photocatalytic ability have been well reviewed and reported [48–50]. The mechanism can also be expressed as the following equations:
CV + hν CV*(2).
CV*+ CdS CV .
++ (e-) CdS(3).
CdS + hν (e-+h+) CdS(4).
CdS (e-) + C-dots (e-) C-dots(5).
e- (CdS + C-dots) + O2 O2
.- (6) OH–+ h+
. OH(7).
CV/ CV .
++ (h+, O2
.- , . OH) Photodegraded Products(8).

This work demonstrates the formation of bio-derived C-dots modified hexagonal wurtzite phase CdS nanospheres via hydrothermal technique to generate a heterostructure nanophotocatalyst. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution studies via water splitting reaction as developed C-dots@CdS-6 evinced yielded 1582 µmolg−1 of H2 in three hours which is very much appreciable to be reported in the absence of any co-catalysts. Additionally, C-dots@CdS-6 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic CV dye degradation rate of 97.3 % in 120 min using the optimised catalytic dosage of 30 mg under neutral conditions. The detailed kinetics evaluation of the dye degradation using C-dots@CdS fitted well with the pseudo-second order kinetics and found to be stable up to three consequent cycles. Additionally, the photoluminescence and electrochemical impedence spectral investigation supports the prominent role played by C-dots in the improved photocatalytic performance of C-dots@CdS-6 which can be attributed to the restriction of electron-hole recombination via trapping and promoting photo-excited charge carrier transfers within the system. The current work opens up new avenues of design and development of bio-derived, economic, biocompatible C-dots based multicomponent photocatalytic systems for waste water treatment and hydrogen generation via water splitting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors wish to acknowledge the project sanctioned by Visvesvaraya Technological University under Research Grants Scheme (No: VTU/BGM/Aca/A-12/VTU RGS/DIS-Chemistry/2021-22/5859/2) and the management of M S Ramaiah Institute of Technology for extending the characterization facilities through Centre for Advanced Materials Technology.
